name: hero-section
role: Top-level <section> wrapper for the typography-only hero. The simplest of the 5 hero variants — no media, no illustration, no animation; just typography and CTAs. Used as the flagship marketing hero where the headline is the main visual element. Four alignment registers (centered / left-aligned / right-aligned / split-anchor). Three sizes (compact / default / oversized). Five backgrounds (surface / surface-elevated / surface-recessed / accent-recede / dark).

semanticStructure: <hero-headline id='hero-headline-id'> — h1, the big display type

registers.centered: Everything center-aligned. The canonical marketing hero — Stripe, Linear, Vercel home pages.
registers.left-aligned: Everything left-aligned. Used in editorial / blog-style heroes, docs landings, B2B SaaS where content density matters.
registers.right-aligned: Less common; used in art-direction-heavy marketing where the right edge anchors against a media element (which would arrive in hero-brand-photo or hero-video patterns).
registers.split-anchor: Headline + subhead on the left; action cluster on the right. Used on dense flagship pages where the CTA needs to be visually balanced against the headline.

sizes.compact: Lower padding-y. Used as page-internal heros (e.g., feature subpages, blog category landings).
sizes.oversized: Hero takes ~80% of viewport height. Used on flagship marketing home pages with no fold cut-off.

backgrounds.surface: Default — same as page surface, no distinction.
backgrounds.accent-recede: Tinted accent background. Used to colour-anchor the hero.
backgrounds.dark: Inverted — accent-coloured ground with text-on-accent foreground. Used for high-impact marketing.

ariaPatterns.ariaLabelledby: <section aria-labelledby> points to the hero-headline's id. Gives the section a programmatic name.
ariaPatterns.h1Discipline: hero-headline uses <h1>. Per page convention, only ONE <h1> per page; hero-section IS the h1 anchor for marketing pages.

name: hero-eyebrow
role: Small uppercase pre-heading above the main headline. Used to categorise ('PRODUCT LAUNCH') or signal context ('NEW IN GALLEY'). Renders as a small text strip in --text-recede with tracking-wide letter-spacing.

ariaPatterns.notHeading:
Eyebrow is NOT a heading element (not h1/h2/etc) — it's just

. The h1 is the hero-headline below. Marking the eyebrow as a heading would break the page outline.

name: hero-headline
role: The big display-type headline. The visual centrepiece of the typography-only hero. h1 by default — and per convention, the ONE h1 per marketing page. Uses --font-display + balance text-wrap. Sizes: 3xl (compact), 4xl (default), 5xl-and-up (oversized).

meta.textWrapBalance: Apply text-wrap: balance — critical for hero headlines where line breaks are aesthetic.
meta.maxWidthChoices: Headlines have max-width in ch units. 20ch for left/right-aligned (encourages 2-3 line headlines); 30ch for centered (allows wider single-line treatment).
copyGuidelines: Lead with verb or noun, not adjective. 'Track every charge.' beats 'Comprehensive subscription tracking.'
Two-line headlines are stronger than one-liners — they create rhythm and let the second line punch.
Avoid stacked clauses. 'Five tools that compose.' is one breath; 'Five tools that compose, with one subscription' is two.

name: hero-subhead
role: Supporting subtitle below the headline. 1-2 sentences max. Used to clarify or elaborate on the headline — the headline is the punch; the subhead is the proof. max 60ch line length via inline max-width.

copyGuidelines: Be specific. 'Five tools, one subscription, one sign-in' beats 'A modern productivity suite.'
Reference the proof or mechanism. 'Built on Quoin so every tool feels at home in whatever aesthetic you choose.'
Don't repeat the headline. Subhead extends; it doesn't restate.

name: hero-actions
role: CTA cluster below the headline / subhead. Composes <action-button> from pattern-button-system. Typical pattern: 1 primary action ('Start free trial') + 1 secondary or ghost action ('View pricing'). Up to 3 CTAs for extended; more than that and the hero gets diluted.

registers.primary-only: Single CTA. Used when there's only one conversion path or when the hero is unusually focused.
registers.primary-plus-secondary: Primary + ghost or secondary. The 80% recommendation. 'Start free trial' (primary) + 'View pricing' (ghost). Reading flow: highest intent right-most in LTR.
registers.extended: Up to 3 CTAs (e.g., 'Start trial' + 'Watch demo' + 'Talk to sales'). Used on flagship marketing where multiple personas need a clear entry point.

meta.buttonSizing: Hero CTAs default to size='md' from button-system. Use size='lg' for oversized hero size. Don't mix sizes within a single cluster.

ariaPatterns.groupRole: <div role='group' aria-label='Hero actions'> wraps the cluster.
ariaPatterns.ariaLabelOverride: If the hero is themed around a specific concept (e.g., 'Free trial'), the aria-label can be more specific: aria-label='Sign up actions'.

name: hero-meta
role: Optional small-print row below the CTAs. Used for social proof ('Trusted by 14,000 teams'), disclaimers ('No credit card required'), or compliance notes ('SOC 2 Type II'). Small typography, --text-recede colour. May include inline icons (security badge, customer logos).

registers.disclaimer: Single-line legal / compliance note ('No credit card required · 14-day trial · Cancel anytime').
registers.social-proof: Brief social-proof phrasing ('Trusted by 14,000 teams · Featured in TechCrunch').
registers.trust-badges: Inline row of small logos or compliance icons (SOC 2 / GDPR / ISO 27001). May render as a flex row of elements with explicit alt-text.

ariaPatterns.trustBadgeAltText: Trust-badge images must carry alt-text naming the certification ('SOC 2 Type II compliant'). Don't use aria-hidden — these are meaningful credentials.
ariaPatterns.social-proofMetric: Social-proof claims should be verifiable (the number should be real). If approximate, indicate ('over 14,000' not '14,000').
